content:
# Genesis Chapter 14 — Disagreement Report
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Chapter:** Genesis 14 (War of Kings / Melchizedek, 24 verses)
**Translators in Council:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Date of Arbitration:** 2026-03-04

---

## Summary

Genesis 14 presented **ten disagreements** across the three translators — one more than Genesis 13, reflecting the chapter's greater linguistic difficulty, the density of its hapax and rare vocabulary, and the extraordinary theological complexity of the Melchizedek episode. The disagreements concentrated in five areas: (1) the rendering of the hapax חֲנִיכִים (chanichim, "trained men") at verse 14, (2) the central crux of קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ ("possessor" or "creator" of heaven and earth) at verses 19 and 22, (3) the preposition in כֹּהֵן לְאֵל עֶלְיוֹן ("priest *of* / priest *to* God Most High") at verse 18, (4) the verse presentation of the Melchizedek blessing (vv. 19b–20a), (5) the subject ambiguity of the tithe clause at verse 20, and several smaller disagreements in phrasing and register.

The degree of agreement on the theological core was notable: all three translators independently identified the chapter's tripartite structure, the two-kings-one-valley contrast, and the significance of Abram's refusal of the king of Sodom as a theological statement about the source of his wealth. All three rejected the LXX Vaticanus's omission of the Tetragrammaton at verse 22. The major crux — "possessor" versus "creator" for קֹנֵה — was the one disagreement where PNEUMA stood alone against EMET and LOGOS, and where the council's deliberation was most extended.

---

## Disagreement Index

| # | Verse(s) | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|----------|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | 14:1 | Opening formula — "And it was" / "And it came to pass" | "And it was in the days of" | "And it came to pass in the days of" | Restructured (no formula preserved) | **"And it came to pass in the days of"** |
| 2 | 14:12 | Lot's relationship to Abram — genealogical phrase vs. "nephew" | "son of Abram's brother" | "son of Abram's brother" | "Abram's nephew" | **"son of Abram's brother" (v.12); "kinsman" (v.14)** |
| 3 | 14:13 | בַּעֲלֵי בְרִית — "covenant-allies" vs. "allies" | "covenant-allies" | "allies of Abram" | "Abram's allies" | **"covenant-allies"** |
| 4 | 14:14 | חֲנִיכָיו — "trained men" + "mustered" / "led out" | "mustered his trained men" | "led out his trained men" | "called out his trained fighting men" | **"led out his trained men"** |
| 5 | 14:17 | מֵהַכּוֹת — "from striking" / "from the defeat of" / "from his victory over" | "from striking Chedorlaomer" | "from the defeat of Chedorlaomer" | "from his victory over Chedorlaomer" | **"from defeating Chedorlaomer"** |
| 6 | 14:18 | כֹּהֵן לְאֵל עֶלְיוֹן — "priest of" vs. "priest to" | "a priest to God Most High" | "priest of God Most High" | "priest of God Most High" | **"a priest of God Most High"** |
| 7 | 14:19–20 | Verse format for Melchizedek's blessing | Prose with verse markers | Prose | Verse format (poetic presentation) | **Verse format adopted** |
| 8 | 14:19, 22 | קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ — "possessor" vs. "Creator" | "possessor of heaven and earth" | "possessor of heaven and earth" | "Creator of heaven and earth" | **"possessor of heaven and earth"** |
| 9 | 14:20 | וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ מַעֲשֵׂר — subject ambiguity preserved vs. resolved | "and he gave him a tithe of all" | "And Abram gave him a tenth of everything" | "And Abram gave him a tenth of everything" | **"And he gave him a tithe of everything"** (ambiguity preserved) |
| 10 | 14:23 | שְׂרוֹךְ-נַעַל — "sandal-thong" vs. "sandal strap" | "sandal-thong" | "sandal strap" | "sandal strap" | **"sandal strap"** |

---

## Disagreement Log — Full Detail

---

### DISAGREEMENT 1 — Verse 1: Opening Formula

**Hebrew:** וַיְהִי בִּימֵי אַמְרָפֶל (*vayyehi bimey Amraphel*, "and it was in the days of Amraphel")
**Nature:** Formulaic — the standard biblical era-setting formula and its English rendering
**Severity:** Low — affects the register and historical framing of the chapter opening

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "And it was in the days of Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of Goiim, they made war..." | Literal: preserves וַיְהִי as "and it was"; the four-king list is kept continuous |
| LOGOS | "And it came to pass in the days of Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of Goiim, that these kings made war..." | Standard formal rendering of וַיְהִי בִּימֵי; "it came to pass" for the formulaic use of וַיְהִי; adds "that" to connect the list to the main clause |
| PNEUMA | "In the days of Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of Goiim — these four kings went to war against..." | Restructures entirely: drops the opening וַיְהִי; moves the kings into a temporal phrase; adds "these four kings" for clarity |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "And it came to pass in the days of Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of Goiim" — LOGOS is followed.

The formula וַיְהִי בִּימֵי is a recognizable biblical convention that carries its own significance — it signals historical chronicle, places the narrative within a named era, and gives the chapter its archival register. EMET's "and it was in the days of" is the most literal but reads slightly flat in English. PNEUMA's restructuring (dropping the formula entirely) loses the distinctive register that sets Genesis 14 apart from the surrounding narrative; the opening וַיְהִי is not decorative but genre-defining. LOGOS's "and it came to pass" is the established English equivalent for the formulaic וַיְהִי and is adopted. The council also adopts LOGOS's connective "that" linking the king list to the main clause — it supplies a grammatical hinge that the Hebrew's list structure implies but does not state.

---

### DISAGREEMENT 2 — Verse 12 and Verse 14: Lot's Relationship to Abram

**Hebrew v. 12:** בֶּן-אֲחִי אַבְרָם (*ben-akhi Avram*, "son of the brother of Abram")
**Hebrew v. 14:** אָחִיו (*akhiv*, "his brother/kinsman")
**Nature:** Lexical — whether to preserve the genealogical specification at v. 12 and how to render the flexible אָח at v. 14
**Severity:** Low-moderate — affects precision of the relationship; the issue is whether to resolve the Hebrew idiom into the modern English equivalent

| Translator | Rendering (v. 12) | Rendering (v. 14) | Rationale |
|------------|-------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| EMET | "the son of Abram's brother" | "his kinsman" | Preserves the genealogical phrase at v. 12; uses "kinsman" for the broad אָח at v. 14 |
| LOGOS | "the son of Abram's brother" | "his kinsman" | Full agreement with EMET |
| PNEUMA | "Abram's nephew" | "his nephew" | Renders the relationship into its precise modern English equivalent; consistent use of "nephew" throughout |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "the son of Abram's brother" at verse 12; "his kinsman" at verse 14.

PNEUMA's "nephew" is accurate and natural in contemporary English — Lot is indeed Abram's nephew (the son of Haran, Abram's deceased brother, per Genesis 11:27–28). The precision is not wrong. However, the Hebrew's use of the genealogical phrase בֶּן-אֲחִי ("son of the brother of") at verse 12 is structurally intentional: it reminds the reader, at the moment of Lot's capture, of the precise kinship bond that obliges Abram to act. The phrase carries legal and covenantal weight — the nephew relationship in the ancient Near East created specific obligations of redemption (compare the goel concept). Replacing it with "nephew" in verse 12 renders the relationship correct but strips the genealogical specification that activates the obligation.

At verse 14, the Hebrew אָחִיו ("his kinsman/brother") is the flexible term that Abram himself uses to describe his bond to Lot in 13:8 ("for we are kinsmen/brothers"). "Kinsman" preserves that connection and is the established SOPHIA rendering for the broad Hebrew אָח in relational contexts.

---

### DISAGREEMENT 3 — Verse 13: בַּעֲלֵי בְרִית — "Covenant-Allies" vs. "Allies"

**Hebrew:** וְהֵם בַּעֲלֵי בְרִית-אַבְרָם (*vehem ba'aley brit-Avram*, "and they were lords/masters of Abram's covenant")
**Nature:** Lexical — whether to preserve the formal covenantal vocabulary
**Severity:** Moderate — the phrase is unique in the patriarchal narratives and marks a recognized formal alliance structure

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "and they were Abram's covenant-allies" | בַּעֲלֵי בְרִית is a formal alliance term; "covenant-allies" preserves the covenantal dimension that distinguishes this from mere friendship |
| LOGOS | "They were allies of Abram" | "Allies" is the natural English equivalent; the covenant dimension is implicit in "allies"; "covenant-allies" risks sounding archaic |
| PNEUMA | "these men were Abram's allies" | Agrees with LOGOS in substance; adds "these men" for clarity of reference |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "and they were Abram's covenant-allies" — EMET is followed.

The phrase בַּעֲלֵי בְרִית ("lords/masters of a covenant" or "covenant-holders") is a formal legal-diplomatic term that appears uniquely in Genesis 14:13 in the patriarchal narratives. It designates a recognized alliance relationship with covenantal obligations — more than mere friendship, more than temporary military cooperation. The word בְּרִית (brit, "covenant") is the same word that designates the Abrahamic covenant itself; its use here for the alliance with the three Amorite brothers signals that Abram's relationship with these men has a formal, binding character. LOGOS's "allies" and PNEUMA's "allies" are accurate but lose the formal covenantal dimension that the Hebrew preserves. The council follows EMET: "covenant-allies."

---

### DISAGREEMENT 4 — Verse 14: The Hapax חֲנִיכִים and the Deployment Verb

**Hebrew:** וַיָּרֶק אֶת-חֲנִיכָיו יְלִידֵי בֵיתוֹ (*vayyaraq et-chanichav yeldei veito*, "and he poured out / drew out / mustered his trained men, the born-ones of his house")
**Nature:** Lexical (two sub-issues) — (a) the hapax חֲנִיכִים, (b) the verb וַיָּרֶק
**Severity:** Moderate — the hapax cannot be rendered with certainty; the verb's metaphorical force is significant

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "he mustered his trained men, the born-ones of his house, 318" | "Mustered" for וַיָּרֶק (Hiphil of רִיק = "to pour out, empty out"; military deployment sense); "trained men" for chanichim; preserves "born-ones of his house" as a separate phrase |
| LOGOS | "he led out his trained men, born in his household, 318" | "Led out" for וַיָּרֶק; "trained men" for chanichim; "born in his household" integrates the phrase more smoothly |
| PNEUMA | "he called out his trained fighting men — 318 of them, all born into his household" | "Called out" for וַיָּרֶק; adds "fighting" to "trained men"; restructures the number and household phrase with a dash |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "he led out his trained men — born in his household, three hundred and eighteen" — a blend of LOGOS and EMET.

**On וַיָּרֶק:** The Hiphil of רִיק ("to empty out, pour out") is used elsewhere of drawing a sword from a scabbard (Psalm 35:3; Ezekiel 28:7) — a vivid metaphor of total deployment. "Mustered" (EMET) is the standard military English but misses the pouring-out/emptying metaphor. "Led out" (LOGOS) is clear and natural but also misses the metaphor. "Called out" (PNEUMA) is too weak for a military deployment. The council adopts "led out" as the most transparent English military equivalent, noting in the consensus notes that the underlying Hebrew metaphor suggests total commitment — Abram empties his household's fighting capacity into the pursuit.

**On חֲנִיכִים:** All three translators converge on "trained men" — the council confirms this as the best rendering of the hapax given context and cognate evidence. PNEUMA's addition of "fighting" is not adopted: the military context already implies fighting; adding the word goes beyond the hapax's lexical evidence.

**On structure:** The council adopts a dash to separate "trained men" from the appositional phrase "born in his household" — following PNEUMA's formatting instinct — since the two elements are distinct: the trained-men designation (chanichim) and the household-birth specification (yeldei veito). The number "three hundred and eighteen" follows in full — EMET and LOGOS both render the Hebrew's characteristic units-before-hundreds order; PNEUMA's restructuring is not needed.

---

### DISAGREEMENT 5 — Verse 17: מֵהַכּוֹת — "From Striking" / "From Defeating" / "From His Victory Over"

**Hebrew:** אַחֲרֵי שׁוּבוֹ מֵהַכּוֹת אֶת-כְּדָרְלָעֹמֶר (*akharey shuvo mehhakkot et-Kedarlaomer*, "after his return from striking Chedorlaomer")
**Nature:** Lexical — the rendering of the Hiphil infinitive construct מֵהַכּוֹת
**Severity:** Low — affects tone more than meaning; the substance is agreed

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "from striking Chedorlaomer" | הַכּוֹת is the Hiphil infinitive of נָכָה ("to strike"); "striking" is the literal equivalent; accurate to the Hebrew action word |
| LOGOS | "from the defeat of Chedorlaomer" | Nominalizes the infinitive; "defeat" is the result of striking; more natural English in the context of a military narrative |
| PNEUMA | "from his victory over Chedorlaomer" | Adds "his victory" — an interpretive rendering that adds the victor's perspective; goes beyond the Hebrew which simply says "from striking" |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "from defeating Chedorlaomer" — a blend, taking LOGOS's sense in verbal rather than nominal form.

EMET's "from striking" preserves the Hebrew infinitive faithfully but "striking" in English can imply a single blow rather than the comprehensive military victory the text describes. PNEUMA's "his victory over" imports a perspective (Abram's triumph framed from his point of view) that the Hebrew's more neutral "from striking" does not supply. LOGOS's "the defeat of Chedorlaomer" is accurate but nominalizes unnecessarily — the Hebrew has an infinitive, not a noun. The council adopts "from defeating Chedorlaomer" — a verbal form that carries the completeness of military defeat without adding the interpretive "his victory" and without the overly literal "striking." The LXX (καταστρέψαι, "to overthrow, to turn upside down") supports a rendering that captures full defeat rather than a single strike.

---

### DISAGREEMENT 6 — Verse 18: כֹּהֵן לְאֵל עֶלְיוֹן — "Priest *of*" vs. "Priest *to*"

**Hebrew:** וְהוּא כֹּהֵן לְאֵל עֶלְיוֹן (*vehu kohen le'el elyon*, "and he was priest to/of God Most High")
**Nature:** Grammatical — the force of the preposition לְ in the priestly designation
**Severity:** Low — the difference is subtle but real

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "and he was a priest to God Most High" | Preserves the preposition לְ in its directional sense: a priest who serves *toward* / *for* God Most High; the verb of priestly service is always directed *to* the deity |
| LOGOS | "Now he was priest of God Most High" | Standard English idiom: "priest of [deity]" designates priestly office by the deity worshipped; "of" is the natural English genitive for priestly designation |
| PNEUMA | "priest of God Most High" | Agrees with LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "and he was a priest of God Most High" — LOGOS and PNEUMA carry the majority; the article "a" is retained from EMET.

The Hebrew לְ before אֵל עֶלְיוֹן is a directional/relational preposition that can be rendered "to," "for," or "of" in English depending on context. In the context of priestly designation ("priest of [deity]"), English consistently uses "of" as the genitive of association — "priest of Baal," "priest of Dagon," "priest of God Most High." This is not a departure from the Hebrew; it is the natural English equivalent for the semantic function the preposition serves here. EMET's "priest to" is accurate but sounds slightly liturgical or directional in a way that the Hebrew's priestly-office usage does not specifically require. The council adopts "priest of" while retaining the indefinite article "a" that EMET supplies — "a priest of God Most High," since Melchizedek is being introduced and the article correctly signals the noun phrase as a description.

---

### DISAGREEMENT 7 — Verses 19b–20a: Verse Format for Melchizedek's Blessing

**Hebrew:** בָּרוּךְ אַבְרָם לְאֵל עֶלְיוֹן ... וּבָרוּךְ אֵל עֶלְיוֹן אֲשֶׁר-מִגֵּן צָרֶיךָ בְּיָדֶךָ
**Nature:** Presentational — whether to render the two-part blessing as prose or verse
**Severity:** Moderate — the format shapes the reader's experience of the most liturgically significant passage in the chapter

| Translator | Rendering Format | Rationale |
|------------|-----------------|-----------|
| EMET | Continues in prose narrative with verse markers (bold **14:19**, **14:20**); the blessing is quoted within the prose sentence structure | Maintains the narrative continuity of the passage; the blessing is part of the prose narration; no clear poetic indicators in the Hebrew (no parallelism markers, no meter) |
| LOGOS | Prose, with the blessing embedded in the verse-numbered prose text | Same as EMET; LOGOS notes the two-part structure in the notes but does not break into verse format in the translation text |
| PNEUMA | Verse format: the two lines are set out as a liturgical poem within the prose narrative | The bipartite structure — two parallel units each opening with בָּרוּךְ ("blessed") — has the character of a liturgical pronouncement; the verse format signals to the reader that they are encountering a different register (priestly speech) within the narrative |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** Verse format — PNEUMA's formal instinct is adopted.

All three translators identified the bipartite structure of the blessing. The two units are formally parallel: each opens with בָּרוּךְ ("blessed be"), each names a subject (Abram; God Most High), and each uses a participial or relative clause. This is the structure of liturgical blessing in the Hebrew Bible — compare the two-part blessings of Naomi in Ruth 2:19–20, and the paired blessing formulas of the Psalter. The bipartite blessing is not mere prose; it is elevated speech in a recognized liturgical form.

PNEUMA correctly identifies this and formats accordingly. EMET and LOGOS recognize the structure in their notes but do not reflect it in the translation text. The council rules that the translation text should make this liturgical elevation visible to the reader through formatting — as the bipartite blessing is one of the most formally significant moments in the entire patriarchal narrative, and the verse presentation serves the text's own register rather than imposing an alien form upon it.

---

### DISAGREEMENT 8 — Verses 19 and 22: קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ — "Possessor" or "Creator"

**Hebrew:** קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ (*qoneh shamayim va'arets*)
**Nature:** Lexical — the semantic range of קָנָה (qanah) between "possess/acquire" and "create/beget"
**Severity:** High — this is the central lexical crux of Genesis 14, and the choice carries significant theological implications

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "possessor of heaven and earth" | The lexically more frequent meaning of קָנָה is "to acquire/possess"; the participial form (ongoing state) better suits "possessor" than "creator"; the chapter's vocabulary is saturated with possession/acquisition language (רְכוּשׁ throughout); the EMET does not resolve ambiguities the Hebrew leaves open |
| LOGOS | "possessor of heaven and earth" | Agrees with EMET; additionally: the literary context of the chapter (everything is about who possesses what) resonates with "possessor"; the LXX's "creator" reading is ancient and weighty but the participial form and literary context favor "possessor" |
| PNEUMA | "Creator of heaven and earth" | The LXX Vaticanus (ὃς ἔκτισεν τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν, "who created heaven and earth") and the Vulgate (*qui creavit*) both resolve the crux toward creation; the Ugaritic cognate (*qny 'rs*, "creator of the earth") supports the creative sense; in context, the more powerful theological ground for the blessing is that God *made* everything — his authority over enemies is ontological, not merely proprietary; "Creator" is the stronger statement in the context of a blessing after military victory |

**Evidence Summary:**

*For "possessor":*
- The dominant meaning of קָנָה throughout the Hebrew Bible (Genesis 4:1; 25:10; 47:19–20 — commercial/acquisitive contexts)
- The participial form קֹנֵה describes an ongoing state, not a past act of creation (contrast Genesis 1:1 which uses the perfect בָּרָא for creation)
- The chapter's sustained vocabulary of possession: רְכוּשׁ (possessions) appears in vv. 11, 12, 16, 21; the chapter is a prolonged meditation on who owns what
- Vulgate: *possessori caeli et terrae* at first occurrence (though Vulgate also uses *creavit* in some forms)
- Targum Onkelos: renders the passage in a sense closer to "possessor/owner"

*For "creator":*
- LXX Vaticanus: ὃς ἔκτισεν τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν (unambiguous "created," using κτίζω with no commercial nuance) — ancient and authoritative
- Ugaritic *qny 'rs* ("creator of the earth") applied to El, the high deity
- Deuteronomy 32:6: קָנֶךָ ("the one who created/acquired you") — clearly in a creative sense
- Proverbs 8:22: קָנָנִי ("the LORD created/acquired me") — Wisdom's speech at creation
- Vulgate: *qui creavit caelum et terram* (in some manuscript traditions)
- PNEUMA's theological argument: the creative ground is the more powerful basis for the blessing — God's authority over enemies flows from his originating act, not merely his ownership

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "possessor of heaven and earth" — EMET and LOGOS carry the majority.

The council's deliberation on this crux was the most sustained of any disagreement in Genesis 14. The council acknowledges the full weight of PNEUMA's reasoning and of the LXX's reading.

The council rules for "possessor" on three grounds stated in full in Council Note 10 of the consensus translation: (1) the participial form (ongoing state, not past event) better suits the possessive than the creative semantic; (2) the chapter's own vocabulary context — the sustained meditation on possession and acquisition — resonates with "possessor" in a way that "creator" does not specifically exploit; (3) "possessor of heaven and earth" does not exclude the creative act — one does not become possessor of all things except by having made them.

The council emphasizes that PNEUMA's ruling is not wrong — it is a legitimate, ancient, and theologically sophisticated resolution of the crux. The LXX's "creator" has the authority of the oldest surviving Greek translation and reflects an exegetical tradition as old as the text's transmission. The SOPHIA note preserves both readings for the reader, who may hold the full semantic range open.

---

### DISAGREEMENT 9 — Verse 20: The Subject of the Tithe Clause

**Hebrew:** וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ מַעֲשֵׂר מִכֹּל (*vayyitten-lo ma'aser mikkol*, "and he gave him a tithe of all")
**Nature:** Grammatical — the subject of the third-person singular verb is ambiguous
**Severity:** Moderate — whether to resolve the ambiguity in the translation text or to preserve it

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "and he gave him a tithe of all" | The Hebrew is grammatically ambiguous; EMET preserves the ambiguity exactly; context makes the direction (Abram → Melchizedek) clear without resolving it in the text |
| LOGOS | "And Abram gave him a tenth of everything" | Resolves the ambiguity by naming Abram as subject; this is the reading of the Epistle to the Hebrews (7:2, 4) and virtually all ancient translations; the resolution is warranted by context |
| PNEUMA | "And Abram gave him a tenth of everything" | Agrees with LOGOS; the context makes Abram the giver obvious; "tenth" rather than "tithe" for accessibility |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "And he gave him a tithe of everything" — EMET's ambiguity-preservation is adopted; "tithe" is retained over "tenth."

This is the same type of decision the council has faced with other ambiguous pronouns across the Genesis chapters. The council's consistent principle has been to preserve the Hebrew's grammatical structure in the translation text and to resolve (where resolution is clear) in the accompanying notes. The Hebrew וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ does not specify its subject; the context makes Abram the giver unmistakably clear. The notes confirm this direction. The translation text preserves what the Hebrew has.

**"Tithe" vs. "tenth":** LOGOS and PNEUMA both render "a tenth of everything." EMET and the council prefer "tithe" — the word מַעֲשֵׂר (ma'aser) is a technical term with a specific religious and institutional history; it is not merely a mathematical fraction. "A tenth" is numerically correct but "tithe" carries the covenantal and cultic weight that ma'aser accumulates through the biblical text. This is its first appearance in the Bible; "tithe" introduces the term with its full institutional resonance rather than reducing it to a fraction.

---

### DISAGREEMENT 10 — Verse 23: שְׂרוֹךְ-נַעַל — "Sandal-Thong" vs. "Sandal Strap"

**Hebrew:** שְׂרוֹךְ-נַעַל (*serokh-na'al*, "thong/strap/lace of a sandal")
**Nature:** Lexical — the best English equivalent for a sandal-fastening element
**Severity:** Low — the choice between synonyms; the merism is preserved by all three

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "sandal-thong" | שְׂרוֹךְ (serokh) is a leather strap or thong; "sandal-thong" is a traditional English Bible rendering (cf. Genesis 14:23 in many older translations); accurate to the material |
| LOGOS | "sandal strap" | "Sandal strap" is more natural in contemporary English; "thong" has acquired an unintended modern English colloquial meaning that can distract the reader; "strap" is cleaner |
| PNEUMA | "sandal strap" | Agrees with LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "sandal strap" — LOGOS and PNEUMA carry the majority.

EMET correctly notes that "sandal-thong" is the traditional rendering with a long history in English Bible translation. However, the English word "thong" has acquired a dominant contemporary colloquial meaning (a style of undergarment) that has largely displaced its older meaning (a leather strap or fastening). The council recognizes that translation must serve readers in their present linguistic context: "sandal strap" is the cleaner, more transparent contemporary English equivalent that does not risk reader distraction. "Sandal strap" is adopted without qualification. The LXX Vaticanus renders σφαιρωτῆρος ὑποδήματος ("clasp/buckle of a sandal") — a similar item of footwear fastening — confirming the merism's application to the most trivial element of footwear.

---

## Notes on EMET's Textual Contribution

EMET provided the most detailed engagement with the primary manuscripts and LXX variants for this chapter, producing the complete MT/LXX comparative table that documents eight significant divergences including the central "possessor" vs. "creator" crux at verse 19 and the major textual variant (absence of YHWH) at verse 22. EMET's analysis of the hapax חֲנִיכִים — tracing its etymology through the root חָנַךְ to Hanukkah and the cognate Egyptian ḥnkw — is the most precise lexical analysis in the chapter. EMET's discussion of the merism at verse 23 (thread to sandal-thong as the ancient equivalent of "not a penny") and of Abram's theological grounds for refusal is adopted in full in Council Note 13. EMET's notes on the Genesis Apocryphon (1QapGen), the 11QMelchizedek scroll, the Samaritan Pentateuch (agreement with MT throughout), and Targum Onkelos are incorporated into the consensus canonical note. EMET's key contribution on verse 22 — the observation that the LXX's omission of YHWH may reflect "a scribal adjustment to harmonize with Melchizedek's non-YHWH formulation in verses 19–20, or the standard LXX practice of sometimes avoiding the Tetragrammaton" — is adopted in the consensus note as the clearest framing of that major variant.

---

## Notes on PNEUMA's Theological Contribution

PNEUMA provided the most sustained theological and literary analysis of the chapter, particularly on: (1) the Melchizedek figure as an embodiment of priestly presence already at the future holy city — "he is already there, priest and king, in Salem, with bread and wine, when Abram arrives from his night battle in the north"; (2) the sacramental resonance of the bread and wine — "a priest bringing food and drink to a returning warrior was a ritual act of welcome and blessing" — and its relationship to the later Eucharistic tradition; (3) the contrast between Melchizedek's gift (embedding Abram in the story of El Elyon, Creator of heaven and earth) and Sodom's gift (embedding Abram in the story that leads to fire); (4) the theological grammar of the whole chapter — "Abram wins the battle; God wins the praise."

PNEUMA's lone stand for "Creator" over "possessor" at verse 19 is the most theologically argued single position in the chapter's translation debate, and the council takes it seriously even while not adopting it in the translation text. PNEUMA's reading is theologically sound and has the authority of the LXX; the council preserves it in the crux notes as a legitimate reading that the Hebrew intentionally holds open.

PNEUMA's addition of "fighting" to "trained men" (verse 14) was not adopted — it goes beyond the hapax's lexical warrant. PNEUMA's expansion of the halting Lot-as-captive observation ("He is not named as a soldier. He is listed alongside 'all their possessions'") is adopted in Council Note 1 as the most vivid articulation of Lot's narrative position in the chapter.

---

## Notes on LOGOS's Structural Contribution

LOGOS provided the most precise structural mapping of the chapter across three movements, the clearest analysis of the formal MT/LXX comparison, and the most carefully organized translation-decision notes on all ten significant lexical choices. LOGOS's observation on the possessor/creator crux — that the *participial form* (ongoing state) of קֹנֵה better suits "possessor" than "creator," because creation would be a past completed act better rendered by a perfect verb — is the single most precise formal argument in the disagreement and is adopted as the leading rationale for the council's ruling on Disagreement 8. LOGOS's analysis of the bread-and-wine scene ("No translation decision is made on behalf of the later symbolic freight, which the reader may perceive or not") is adopted as the council's stance toward the Eucharistic typology question.

---

## Agreements Across All Three Translators

The following were unanimous across EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA and required no council arbitration:

1. **"The LORD" for יְהוָה** — throughout the chapter.
2. **"Melchizedek king of Salem"** — no dissent on the name or title.
3. **"God Most High" for אֵל עֶלְיוֹן** — unanimous; consistent throughout.
4. **"Bread and wine" for לֶחֶם וָיָיִן** — all three; neither embellished nor reduced.
5. **"Abram the Hebrew"** — all three; the LXX's τῷ περάτῃ is unanimously noted but not followed.
6. **318** — no numerological interpretation; all three render the number straightforwardly.
7. **MT at verse 22 (with יְהוָה)** — all three follow the MT against the LXX's omission of the Tetragrammaton; unanimous.
8. **Abram as giver of the tithe** — all three resolve the grammatical ambiguity the same way; the council preserves the ambiguity in the text while confirming the direction in the notes.
9. **The exception clause (v. 24) as Abram's personal oath not imposed on allies** — all three read it consistently.
10. **The MT throughout** — at every point of MT/LXX divergence, all three translators independently follow the MT on the major issues. The council confirms this unanimity as consistent with the pattern established through Genesis 1–13.
